Welcome to my new blog built with Astro. Here is what to expect and why I started writing.

Welcome! This is the first post on my new blog. I built this site with [Astro](https://astro.build) and deployed it to GitHub Pages — more on that setup in a future post.

## Why a blog?

I've been meaning to write more for a while. Having a dedicated place to put thoughts down (and the slight pressure of knowing it's public) seems like a good motivator. The goal is consistency over perfection.

Topics I plan to cover:

- Web development and things I'm building
- Tools and workflows I find useful
- Interesting things I read or stumble across

## The setup

The site is intentionally simple. Markdown files for content, Astro for static site generation, GitHub Actions for automated deploys. No database, no CMS, no nonsense.

```bash
# Clone and run locally
npm install
npm run dev
```

That's it. Open `http://localhost:4321` and you're off.

## What's next

I'll aim for roughly one post per week, but I won't hold myself to that strictly. Quality over quantity.

Thanks for stopping by.

Why I Switched to Astro for Static Sites
After years of reaching for Next.js for everything, I tried Astro for a content site. I will not go back.

I have a confession: I used to reach for Next.js every time I needed to build anything. API routes? Client components? SSR? Useful sometimes. But for a blog with a few dozen pages of Markdown? Overkill by a wide margin.

Then I tried Astro.

## What makes Astro different

Astro's key idea is **zero JavaScript by default**. Components render to static HTML at build time. You only ship JavaScript when you explicitly opt in with client directives like `client:load` or `client:idle`.

For content-heavy sites, this means:

- Faster page loads (no hydration overhead)
- Better Core Web Vitals scores out of the box
- Simpler mental model — it's just HTML, CSS, and the occasional script

## The content collections API

My favourite feature is [Content Collections](https://docs.astro.build/en/guides/content-collections/). You define a schema for your Markdown frontmatter and Astro validates it at build time.

```typescript
// src/content/config.ts
import { defineCollection, z } from 'astro:content';

const blog = defineCollection({
  schema: z.object({
    title: z.string(),
    pubDate: z.coerce.date(),
    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
  }),
});
```

If a post is missing a required field, the build fails with a clear error. No more typos silently producing broken pages.

## The GitHub Pages deploy

Deploying to GitHub Pages is a single workflow file. Astro even has [official docs](https://docs.astro.build/en/guides/deploy/github/) for it. The `withastro/action` handles install and build; you just push to `main`.

## Would I use something else?

For a full application with auth, a database, or complex client interactivity — yes, probably Next.js or SvelteKit. But for a blog, documentation site, or marketing page? Astro every time.

The tool fits the job.

My Journey With: Music (The Early Years)
After years of playing in bands, I'm finally ready to do my own thing

I remember when I was younger and always wanted to be the voice in the background. I believe that was the reason I went into the Audio Industry for the part time in my life. I remember when I first recorded some audio. It was checking out one of the audio recorders from the cage and looking to get a boom microphone to have it.
